content: Should I put in a testcase, where multiplication results in an integer overflow?
I hope you received enough feedback from the community to improve your contribution. Once you have made the necessary updates, publish your contribution!

Why not include “-” and “/”, since they are at the same level as “+” and “×”? And why not use “*” instead of “×”? This is a good exercise, and I would classify it as medium. There is recursive descent parsing to learn, and we can expand it with “()” and others to explore recursive descent parsing further.

Certain programming languages have an eval function that may be advantageous since they bypass the computation of each natural number from its expanded notation. E.g. in Python,

i=input
r=o=i()=='*'
exec("v=eval(i().replace('x','*'));r=(r+v,r*v)[o];"*int(i()))
print(r)

This doesn't really break the clash; it's just a minor side note :)

General
* The use of "×" in the input may pose parsing challenges to users of some programming languages. Consider replacing it with the letter "x" instead.

Goal
* Please change "F.e" to "e.g.".

* Please specify that the sum should be output when [[op]] is {{+}} and the product when [[op]] is {{*}}.

Input
* Please format "+" and "*" as {{constants}} instead of [[variables]].

* Next n lines: Please format "n" as a [[variable]].

Constraints
* Please change "N" to "n" for consistency with other sections.

* Please remove the second constraint as it repeats what's stated in the Input section.

* Please add:
0 ≤ integer in expanded form < 2⁵³
0 ≤ output < 2⁵³

Stub generator
* Please change 256 to 1.

* Please change 1024 to 64.

Cases
* Test 8: Please delete the trailing newline from the input.

* Please revise Pairs 9 and 10 to address the following:

  • They don't differ significantly from earlier pairs of cases and Pair 11.
  
  • The test and validator in each pair aren't similar enough.
  
  • No cases currently cover multiplication involving medium and large numbers.

* Please add a new pair of cases where op is + and at least one of the integers is 0.

* Please add a new pair of cases where op is * and at least one of the integers is 0.

Thanks.

*I thought that using '×' would be a good lesson for beginners, the difference with x did really trip me up when writting testcases, but if some of the programming languages don't process Unicoide by default, I can see how that might be a bit much for a Coding Challenge.

*As for including Zeroes, at least this definition for Expanded Form does only include POSITIVE INTEGERS https://mathworld.wolfram.com/ExpandedNotation.html
So I am not quite sure about, I mean there is not a lot of reason, not to include Zeroes, especially since Zeroes are already included as multiplicants anyway.

*I think Validator should have the same structure as the accompanying test, just with slightly different numerical values.
I think I would leave this as a clash, and maybe make an exercise with more functions.
How do you expect players to handle the integer overflow?
Just using long

I think this should be fine as long (no pun intended) as you clearly specify the range of expected output. In fact, since you indicate the constraints of the inputs and output to be at most 2^53, I would highly recommend adding more test cases specifically to test handling of larger numbers.
I  put in a testcase where the result is ~ 1 Googol, is that too harsh?
Most languages can only handle up to 64-bit integers (2^64 ~ 9 x 10^18), so 1 Googol (10^100) is probably going to be quite unfair for a clash of code.

Thank you for updating the contribution.

On the point about zeroes: either way is fine with me. If you want to include zero as an operand in some cases, please do so; otherwise, please change the lower limits in the second and third constraints from 0 to 1.

I entirely agree with your point that the validator should have the same structure as the accompanying test, just with slightly different numerical values. As mentioned in my original message, however, Pairs 9 and 10 currently overlap too much with other cases, and also the test and validator aren't closely aligned. I'd therefore suggest replacing them to cover multiplication involving medium and large numbers, which aren't covered in any other cases.

Hope this clarifies.

Summary of current case coverage (by pair, "values" refers to input values):

1. +, small n, small values
2. + & *, small n, small values
3. *, small n, small values (single-digit)
4. *, small n, small values
5. +, small n, medium values
6. +, small n, large values
7. *, small n, large values (validator mixes with small value)
8. *, large n, small values
9. *, small n, small values (validator only single-digit)
10. + & *, large n, small values
11. +, large n, small values
12. *, small n, medium values

Coverage issues:
* Validators are weighted towards multiplication (*), because of Pairs 2 and 10.

* Pair 9 covers the same area already covered by pairs 3 and 4.

* Too many 1 or 2-digit input values.

* Too few medium and large input values, and almost no mixing of magnitudes.
Thanks, I am going to write a script for generating testcases.

Summary of current case coverage:
• +: 6 tests, 4 validator (10)
• *: 6 tests, 8 validators (14)
• 1 ≤ n ≤ 3: 9 tests, 9 validators (18)
• 10 ≤ n ≤ 14: 3 tests, 3 validators (6)
• operands < 2**16: 9.5 tests, 10 validators (19.5)
• 2**16 ≤ operands < 2**32: 1.5 tests, 1.5 validators (3)
• operands ≥ 2**32: 1 test, 0.5 validator (1.5)

Current gaps in coverage:
• Too many cases where 1 ≤ n ≤ 3.
• Too many cases where operands < 2**16.
• No cases where 4 ≤ n ≤ 9.
• Too few cases where operands ≥ 2 **16.
• Almost no cases that mix operand magnitudes.

Since you've written a script for creating test cases, it'd be helpful to adjust it so that it produces more randomised inputs (from random import randrange) and improves overall coverage.

Thanks.
I am going to expand the script, so that it uses random numbers in a range [a,b[ , [b,c[and then produces an validator that is changed in one random digit.
That's good to hear. Just give a word when you've finished updating the cases!
Haven't updated the test cases, but I did write the improved testcase generator.
That's all right. Take your time!

Goal
* Please fix the typo: writen => written.

* Please change "in powers" to "in strictly descending powers".

Constraints
* Please state the upper bound of [[n]].

Cases
* You might still be updating the cases so these comments may be a bit early. Still, I'd like to raise them now so that you have a clearer idea of what cases to generate going forward.

* Just one number (Test 2): Please change [[op]] to * and revise the operand to represent another number of similar magnitude.

* Googolicious (Test 7) and Validator 7: Please revise the cases to ensure that the output remains below 2**53.

* Case coverage still needs improvement. There are too few operands between 2**16 and 2**53 and too many operands below 2**16.

* Please reorder the cases.

  • The first pair should be simple and short (e.g. [[N]] = 3 with small operands) to serve as an illustration and make the problem easier to understand.

  • The remaining pairs should be ordered by increasing complexity
    - from smaller [[N]] to larger [[N]], and
    - from smaller operands to larger operands.

Thanks.
